summary: The notebook demonstrates fine-tuning RibonanzaNet, a pre-trained RNA structure prediction model, to regress C1' atom coordinates directly from nucleotide sequences. It implements a custom PyTorch dataset pipeline, applies a temporal train/test split to mimic structural biology blind testing protocols, and filters sequences based on coordinate completeness and length. The training loop combines a distance-based MAE loss with an SVD-based Procrustes alignment loss to optimize 3D geometry while mitigating rotation and translation invariance. Readers should understand how to adapt a sequence-based RNA model for direct 3D coordinate regression, handle structural data filtering, and leverage rotation-invariant losses for molecular geometry tasks.
competition_flow: Loads RNA sequence and coordinate data, filters and temporally splits it, fine-tunes a pre-trained RibonanzaNet model with a custom coordinate regression head using SVD-aligned MAE loss, and saves the best checkpoint.
data_reading: Reads train_sequences.csv and train_labels.csv via pandas, extracts pdb_id from the ID column, parses x_1, y_1, z_1 columns into float32 numpy arrays, and replaces -1e18 masked values with NaN.
data_processing: Filters sequences with >50% NaN coordinates or length outside [10, 9999999]; crops sequences to a maximum length of 384 during dataset iteration; maps nucleotides (A, C, G, U) to integer tokens; and applies a temporal cutoff split for validation.
cv_strategy: temporal holdout split based on publication dates
insights: Temporal data splitting better reflects real-world structural biology blind testing than random k-fold splits.
Distance-based and SVD-aligned losses help mitigate rotation/translation invariance issues in 3D coordinate regression.
Filtering sequences by coordinate completeness prevents training on heavily masked or incomplete structures.
critical_findings: The author explicitly notes that dRMSD loss cannot distinguish chiral structures due to reflection invariance, implying potential limitations in predicting correct stereochemistry.
